skillName: Order any integers, negatives and decimals
misconceptionSummary: Students order each type separately (all negatives, then all decimals, then integers) and concatenate, breaking the overall order.

lines.text: Grouped negatives first, then decimals, then integers

steps.narration: The student sorted negatives together, then decimals, then integers — treating them as three separate lists. But one is not smaller than all decimals just because it is an integer. Everything must compete on the same scale.
Place all five on one number line regardless of type. The number line does not care whether a value is a negative, a decimal, or an integer — it just shows the true order.

Reading left to right: negative three, negative one, zero point four, one, two point five. The decimal and integer do not form separate groups — zero point four falls between negative one and one because that is its position on the line.

visuals.label: One number line — types don't form separate groups

steps.narration: Negative three, negative one, zero point four, one, two point five. One line, one order — type never overrides position.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: grouping all negatives, then all decimals, then all integers and concatenating the groups. Place everything on one number line — type is irrelevant to order.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Don't sort by type. Every number — negative, decimal, or integer — has one position on the number line. Place them all on one line and read left to right.
